reasoning: The question explicitly asks if South Korea's TFR will increase from 2023 to 2025 (i.e., be strictly > 0.72 in 2025). The most recent official preliminary data from early 2025 reveals that the TFR for 2024 already surged to 0.75, breaking a 9-year negative streak. Furthermore, the number of marriages in 2024 leaped by a massive 14.9% – the fastest pace on record – building on a post-pandemic recovery that began in 2023. Because nearly all births in South Korea occur within wedlock, a surge in marriages overwhelmingly predicts a sustained or further-accelerating increase in births over the following 1-2 years. Therefore, the momentum from the late-2024 marriage and birth acceleration heavily buffers 2025 demographics, significantly insulating the birth rate from dropping back down below the 0.72 threshold even in scenarios where short-term political instability (like the late-2024 martial law incident) introduces some caution among couples. Given the strong 2024 baseline of 0.75 and the powerful forward indicator of the 15% marriage spike, the probability of the 2025 TFR remaining above 0.72 is extremely high.
